event: Genesis — The Chemical Wedding (Marriage of Adam and Eve)
symbols: God in blue robes with raised hand — the divine institution of marriage and blessing of the first couple
trees forming an arch — the Garden of Eden as a natural cathedral
nude Adam and Eve — primordial innocence before the Fall, unashamed
white flowers on the ground — purity and the beauty of unfallen creation
rope belt on God's garment — divine authority constrained by covenantal commitment
description: A flat, stylized composition showing three figures standing in a clearing among brown-trunked trees whose canopies form a natural arch. At center, a bearded figure in a blue robe, black cloak, and red pointed hat raises his right hand in blessing while his left holds something at his waist. On either side stand a nude woman (left) and a nude man (right), both facing the central figure. The ground is covered with small white flowers and green grass. The style is flat, decorative, and folk-art inspired with clear outlines and bright colors.
theological_description: This image depicts the institution of marriage in the Garden of Eden — God Himself bringing Eve to Adam and blessing their union (Genesis 2:22-24). Marriage is thus presented as a divine, not merely human, institution: God is the first celebrant. The trees forming a natural cathedral suggest that before any temple was built, creation itself was the house of worship. Adam and Eve stand in perfect innocence, 'naked and not ashamed' (Genesis 2:25), in a state of grace that will be lost through the Fall. Jesus Himself pointed back to this moment as the foundation of marriage: 'What God has joined together, let no one separate' (Matthew 19:6). The title 'Chemical Wedding' draws on alchemical and mystical traditions where the union of opposites produces transformation — here, the union of man and woman in God's presence creates the foundational human community from which all civilization and all covenant history will flow.

scripture_refs.reason: God bringing Eve to Adam and instituting marriage — the scene directly depicted
They were both naked and not ashamed — the innocence of the first couple
Jesus citing Genesis on marriage: what God has joined together let no one separate
Paul reading the Genesis marriage as a mystery referring to Christ and the Church
